narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.80—well above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 49% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the decision toward rejection. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of past students, focusing on factors like early academic rankings, test scores, and family background to forecast bar success. The model's call hinged most heavily on three key elements of your profile: your undergraduate GPA, your Year 3 school decile ranking, and your LSAT score. Your strong undergrad GPA was the biggest positive force, strongly pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar, as it signals consistent high performance compared to most applicants. However, your Year 3 decile of 1—far below the average of around 5.5—emerged as the top concern, significantly pulling the outlook toward failure by highlighting a drop in school-relative performance later on. Your LSAT score of 22, much lower than the typical 36.7, added further weight toward the failure prediction, reinforcing doubts about standardized test readiness despite your full-time student status, which is common among applicants. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile shows strengths in sustained college grades but weaknesses in later high school rankings and LSAT performance, which often correlate with bar struggles in the data. Your lower-middle family income bracket didn't sway things much here, but it fits a pattern where resource access can subtly influence outcomes. While this rejection stings, especially with your GPA as a highlight, it doesn't define your potential—many applicants rebound by targeting these gaps. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to average levels, and reflect on what led to the low Year 3 decile to build a stronger narrative in future applications. Strengthening these areas could shift future models' predictions in your favor, and exploring programs with holistic reviews might also help. Keep pushing; your undergrad success shows you have the foundation to improve.
